content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660         322-5901         322-6441         322-6444


December 19, 1980


Raymond J. Leonardini
Leonardini & Pathy
717 K Street, Suite 510
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Leonardini:

Thank you for your letter of December 5, 1980, requesting advice regarding your duty under Government Code Section 87100.

The facts, as I understand them from your letter and our discussion, are as follows:

In your capacity as a private attorney, you have represented the California Pipe Trades Council (CPTC) since October 1979. In the course of your representation you have appeared before the State Commission on Housing and Community Development (HCD) in order to oppose HCD's approval of unrestricted use of plastic pipe because of the carcinogenic potential of the ingredients in the pipe. On November 24, 1980, the HCD approved permitting the use of polybutylene plastic pipe for installation in fresh drinking water systems. Before the HCD-approved proposal may become effective, it must be ratified by the Building Standards Commission (BSC) of which you are a member. Your question is whether you are disqualified from participating in the BSC's consideration of the matter and, if so, whether you can play any role in bringing to the HCD's attention what you have learned about the dangers of plastic pipe.

Government Code Section 87100 provides that:

No public official ... shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

Raymond J. Leonardini
December 19, 1980
Page Two


The Act further provides that an official has a financial
interest for the purpose of disqualification,

...if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the public generally, on:

* * *

(c) Any source of income ... aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received
by ... the public official within twelve months prior
to the time when the decision is made.

Gov. Code Section 87103.

Because the CPTC is a source of income,1/ the remaining
question is whether any decision you will make about the use
of plastic pipe in your capacity as a member of the BSC will
have a material financial effect within the meaning of the
statute.

In 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 the Commission has
established guidelines for determining when a decision will
have a material financial effect upon an official's financial
interest. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section (18702(b)(3)(B) provides
that in the case of a source of income of $250 or more an
official must disqualify himself if "there is a nexus between
the governmental decision and the purpose for which the
official receives income...." In other words, the regulation
prevents you from accomplishing as an official what you have
been paid to do as an advocate.

You have also asked whether, in the event you are dis-
qualified, you can address the BSC as a member of the public
in order to bring to the BSC's attention what you conscientiously
believe to be the danger of plastic pipe. As noted above,
Section 87100 prevents a public official from "in any way
attempting to use his official position to influence a
government decision in which he knows ... he has a financial
interest." The Commission has defined this phrase to include

1/ The CPTC is a source of income not only to your
partnership, but to you personally since, as an equal partner,
you own more than a 10% interest in your firm. Government
Code Section 82030(c).

Raymond J. Leonardini
December 19, 1980
Page Three


any attempts to affect an agency decision (2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18700(c)(1)) unless, in pertinent part, the official
"...years to "represent himself or herself on matters related
solely to his or her personal interest." 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18700(E)(1). It is the consensus of staff that
the "matter [of] solely personal interest" exception in
Section 18700(E)(1) refers to those matters about which
there is no coincidental professional interest as well.
Accordingly, it is concluded that you may not personally
address the BSC.

I hope that this answers your question. If you have
any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Very truly yours,

Thomas M. Sobel
Chief, Legal Division

TMS:kp

leonardini & fathy
Attorneys at Law
A Professional Corporation

Raymond J. Leonardini
Richard G. Fathy

717 K Street, Suite 510
Sacramento, CA 95814
(916) 441-1405

Of Counsel
Pierson, Tayer, Kovach
& Bodine

December 5, 1980

Thomas M. Sobel
Fair Political Practices
Commission
1100 K Street, Third Floor
Sacramento, California     95814

Dear Mr. Sobel:

     Pursuant to Government Code Section 83114, subdivision (b), I hereby request that the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) issue its written advice as to whether, in light of Government Code Section 87100, I may sit on the Building Standards Commission (BSC) when it considers an issue in which I have been involved as a private citizen.

     The background information on this matter is straightforward. As a private attorney, I have represented the California Pipe Trades Council (CPTC) since October 1979. In the course of this representation, I have appealed before the State Commission on Housing and Community Development (HCD) and advocated that HCD not consider approving the unrestricted use of plastic pipe in California construction sites until the suspected health risks concerning plastic pipe are thoroughly examined. On November 24, 1980, HCD approved an administrative proposal sanctioning the use of polybutylene plastic pipe for installation in fresh drinking water systems. This HCD approved proposal will soon come before the BSC for final action; in particular, before the proposal may ever have the effect of law, the BSC must first approve it. As noted above, I am one of the commissioners on the BSC.

     My private role as attorney for CPTC will unquestionably continue regardless of how the BSC responds to the HCD action on polybutylene pipe. That is, whatever the BSC decides about polybutylene pipe, I will continue to

Thomas M. Sober
December 5, 1930
Page Two




represent the CPTC on various matters. Accordingly,
I would hope that the FPPC would be of the opinion that
I can properly sit on the BSC, without violating Govern-
ment Code Section 87100, when it considers the proposal
on polybutylene pipe.

     I look forward to your written advice on the
above.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Raymond J. Leonardini

leonardini & fathy
Attorneys at Law
A Professional Corporation

Raymond J. Leonardini
Richard G. Fathy

Of Counsel:
Beeson, Tayer, Kovach
& Bodine

717 K Street, Suite 510
Sacramento, CA 95814
(916) 441-4405

December 5, 1980

Thomas M. Sobel
Fair Political Practices
Commission
1100 K Street, Third Floor
Sacramento, California    95814

Dear Mr. Sobel:

     Pursuant to Government Code Section 83114,
subdivision (b), I hereby request that the Fair Political
Practices Commission (FPPC) issue its written advice as
to whether, in light of Government Code Section 87100, I
may sit on the Building Standards Commission (BSC) when
it considers an issue in which I have been involved as a
private citizen.

     The background information on this matter is
straightforward.  As a private attorney, I have represented
the California Pipe Trades Council (CPTC) since October 1979.
In the course of this representation, I have appeared before
the State Commission on Housing and Community Development
(HCD) and advocated that HCD not consider approving the
unrestricted use of plastic pipe in California construction
sites until the suspected health risks concerning plastic
pipe are thoroughly examined.  On November 24, 1980, HCD
approved an administrative proposal sanctioning the use of
polybutylene plastic pipe for installation in fresh drink-
ing water systems.  This HCD approved proposal will soon
come before the BSC for final action; in particular, before
the proposal may ever have the effect of law, the BSC must
approve it.  As noted above, I am one of the commis-
sioners on the BSC.

     My private role as attorney for CPTC will un-
questionably continue regardless of how the BSC responds
to the HCD action on polybutylene pipe.  That is, whatever
the BSC decides about polybutylene pipe, I will continue to

Thomas M. Sobel
December 5, 1980
Page Two


represent the CPTC on various matters. Accordingly,
I would hope that the FPPC would be of the opinion that
I can properly sit on the BSC, without violating Government Code Section 87100, when it considers the proposal
on polybutylene pipe.

I look forward to your written advice on the
above.

Sincerely,

Raymond J. Leonardini

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance    Administration    Executive Legal    Enforcement    Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5470         322-5501         322-6441         322-6544

December 19, 1980


Raymond J. Leonardini
Leonardini & Pathy
717 K Street, Suite 510
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Leonardini:

Thank you for your letter of December 5, 1980, requesting advice regarding your duties under Government Code Section 87100.

The facts, as I understand them from your letter and our discussion, are as follows:

In your capacity as a private attorney, you have represented the California Pipe Trades Council (CPTC) since October 1979. In the course of your representation you have appeared before the State Commission on Housing and Community Development (HCD) in order to oppose HCD's approval of unrestricted use of plastic pipe because of the carcinogenic potential of the ingredients in the pipe. On November 24, 1980, the HCD approved permitting the use of polybutylene plastic pipe for installation in fresh drinking water systems. Before the HCD-approved proposal any become effective, it must be ratified by the Building Standards Commission (BSC) of which you are a member. Your question is whether you are disqualified from participating in the BSC's consideration of the matter and, if so, whether you can play any role in bringing to the HCD's attention what you have learned about the dangers of plastic pipe.

Government Code Section 87100 provides that:

No public official ... shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

Raymond J. Leonardini
December 19, 1980
Page Two


The Act further provides that an official has a financial
interest for the purposes of disqualification,

   . . . if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the public generally, on:

                         * * *

   (c) Any source of income . . . aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value . . . received
by . . . the public official within twelve months prior
to the time when the decision is made.

                    Gov. Code Section 87103.

Because the CPTC is a source of income,1/ the remaining
question is whether any decision you will make about the use
of plastic pipe in your capacity as a member of the BSC will
have a material financial effect within the meaning of the
statute.

   In 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 the Commission has
assigned guidelines for determining when a decision will
have a material financial effect upon an official's financial
interest. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section (18702(1)(J)(B) provides
that in the case of a source of income of $250 or more an
official must disqualify himself if "there is a nexus between
the governmental decision and the purpose for which the
official receives income. In other words, the regulation
prevents you from accomplishing as an official what you have
been paid to do as an advocate.

   You have also asked whether, in the event you are dis-
qualified, you can address the BSC as a member of the public
in order to bring to the BSC's attention what you conscientiously
believe to be the dangers of plastic pipe. As set forth above,
Section 87100 prevents a public official from in any way
attempt[ing] to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows . . . he has a financial
interest." The Commission has defined this phrase to include

___________
1/ The CPTC is a source of income not only to your
partnership, but to you individually since, as an equal partner,
you own more than a 1% interest in your firm. Government
Code Section 82020(c).

Raymond J. Leonardini
December 19, 1980
Page Three

any attempts to affect an agency decision (2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18700(a)(1); and as in pertinent part the official
appears to "represent himself or herself on matters related
solely to his or her personal interest." (2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18700(f)(1). It is the consensus of the staff that
the "matter [of] solely personal interest" exception in
Section 18700(f)(1) refer to those matters about which
there is no coincidental professional interest as well.
Accordingly, it is concluded that you may not personally
address the BSC.

I hope that this answer your question. If you have
any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Very truly yours,

Thomas M. Schol
Chief, Legal Division
